title: Quiz: Efficient Quantum Natural Gradient
description: Test your understanding of efficient QNG computation methods

questions.question: What is the computational complexity of computing the full QFI matrix for p parameters?
questions.explanation: Computing the full QFI matrix requires estimating p(p+1)/2 unique entries, which is O(p²) quantum circuits.

questions.question: How does block-diagonal approximation reduce complexity?

options.text: By ignoring correlations between parameter groups

questions.explanation: Block-diagonal approximation assumes parameters in different blocks are uncorrelated, allowing each block to be computed independently with fewer total circuits.

questions.question: What method can solve F⁻¹g without explicitly computing F⁻¹?
questions.explanation: Conjugate gradient iteratively solves Fx = g using only matrix-vector products, avoiding explicit matrix inversion.

questions.question: How many function evaluations does SPSA require per gradient estimate?
questions.explanation: SPSA uses only 2 function evaluations (one for +Δ and one for -Δ perturbation), regardless of the number of parameters.

questions.question: Layer-wise QNG computes exact natural gradients by treating layers independently.
questions.explanation: False. Layer-wise QNG is an approximation that ignores cross-layer correlations in the QFI matrix. It's not exact but often accurate enough.

questions.question: What information guides optimal measurement shot allocation?
questions.explanation: Larger QFI eigenvalues indicate more sensitive parameter directions, which benefit from more measurement shots for accurate estimation.

questions.question: For k equal-sized blocks of p/k parameters each, what is the block-diagonal circuit complexity?
questions.explanation: Each block requires O((p/k)²) circuits, and there are k blocks, giving O(k × (p/k)²) = O(p²/k) total circuits.

questions.question: Which are valid approaches to reduce QNG computational cost? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Diagonal approximation, stochastic estimation, and CG solvers all reduce computational cost. Increasing circuit depth doesn't help with QNG computation efficiency.
